# Inference Efficiency {.section-divider}

## Why Speed Matters in Robot Control

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- Control loops have strict timing budgets.
- Slow inference increases reaction delay.
- Contact-rich tasks may require fast corrections.
- Worst-case latency matters more than nice average timing.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/control_loop_latency.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Denoising Steps vs Latency

Let $K$ be the number of denoising steps and $T_{net}$ the cost of one denoising network evaluation:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
T_{infer}
\approx
K \cdot T_{net}
$$
:::

For a policy query, a more realistic budget is:

$$
T_{query}
=
T_{sense}
+
T_{enc}
+
K T_{net}
+
T_{comm}
$$

---

## DDPM Sampling Cost

DDPM sampling uses many small stochastic reverse steps:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
x_{k-1}
=
\mu_\theta(x_k,k)
+
\sigma_k z,
\qquad
z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Benefits:

- faithful to the original probabilistic sampler
- smooth incremental refinement

Costs:

- many network evaluations
- stochasticity can add rollout variability
- hard to fit into fast control loops
:::

::: {.warning}
A sampler that is excellent for offline generation can be too slow for online control.
:::
:::

---

## DDIM for Fewer Steps

DDIM uses a non-Markovian reverse trajectory and can skip diffusion timesteps:

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
- same training objective as DDPM-style noise prediction
- fewer denoising evaluations at inference
- deterministic when the stochasticity parameter is zero
- useful for latency-constrained policy queries
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/ddpm_ddim_comparison.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Same Network, Different Sampler

The trained denoiser is reused:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\epsilon_\theta(x_k,k,c)
\quad
\text{is unchanged}
$$
:::

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
What changes at deployment is the update rule:

- DDPM: many stochastic transitions
- DDIM: fewer, often deterministic jumps
- both operate in the same normalized action space
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/same_network_different_sampler.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Quality, Latency, and Stability

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
Reducing $K$ improves latency, but can change behavior:

- less refinement of the action chunk
- possible lower task success
- possible rougher trajectories
- different closed-loop stability properties

The right sampler is task- and hardware-dependent.
:::

::: {}
![](assets/figures/sampling_steps_latency.svg){width=100%}
:::
:::

---

## Action Chunking and Replanning Frequency

Action chunking amortizes inference cost:

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\text{query once}
\rightarrow
\text{execute } h \text{ actions}
\rightarrow
\text{query again}
$$
:::

Tradeoff:

- larger $h$: lower query frequency, more stale actions
- smaller $h$: more reactive, higher inference burden
- larger $H$: more temporal context, harder sequence modeling

---

## Model-Side Acceleration

Sampler changes are only one option. Other directions include:

- smaller 1D U-Net backbones
- lower-dimensional action parameterizations
- policy distillation into a feed-forward policy
- consistency-style or few-step diffusion samplers
- hardware-aware deployment and compilation

::: {.takeaway}
Acceleration is useful only if the closed-loop robot behavior remains stable, reactive, and task competent.
:::

---

## Slow-Fast Policy Interface

::: {.two-column-balanced}
::: {.compact}
A useful robotics pattern:

- a slower diffusion policy proposes coherent action chunks
- a faster low-level controller tracks or filters commands
- compliance control reacts quickly to measured interaction forces
:::

::: {.equation-card}
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{diffusion policy}
&\rightarrow
\text{reference motion} \\
&\rightarrow
\text{compliant controller}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::
